1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	
47	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
48	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
49	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
50	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
51	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
52	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
53	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
54	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
55	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
56	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
57	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
58	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
59	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
60	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
61	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
62	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
63	
64	\end{theorem}
65	
66	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold pass creates the decl pinned above with
67	% exactly this signature):
68	%   (A B M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
69	%     [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]
70	%     [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
71	%     [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
72	%     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
73	%       Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M)
74	
75	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
76	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
77	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
78	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
79	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
80	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
81	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
82	% there exists a finite filtration
83	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
84	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
85	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
86	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
87	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
88	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
89	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
90	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
91	\begin{proof}
92	
93	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
94	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
95	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
96	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
97	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
98	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
99	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
100	
101	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
102	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
103	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
104	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
105	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
106	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
107	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
108	  for exactly this residue.
109	
110	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
111	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
112	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
113	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
114	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
115	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
116	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
117	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
118	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
119	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
120	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
121	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
122	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
123	\end{proof}
124	
125	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
126	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
127	
128	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
129	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
130	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
131	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
132	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
133	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
134	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
135	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
136	
137	\begin{lemma}[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
138	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
140	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
141	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
142	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
143	\end{lemma}
144	
145	\begin{proof}
146	
147	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
148	  finitely presented, so
149	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
150	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
151	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
152	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
153	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
154	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
155	  \(f := r\).
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
159	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
160	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
161	  \mathlibok
162	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
163	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
164	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
165	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
166	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r\) (of the same finite rank).
167	\end{lemma}
168	
169	\begin{lemma}[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
170	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
172	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
173	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
174	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
175	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
176	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
177	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
178	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
179	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
180	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
181	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
182	  induction).}
183	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
184	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
185	  \(A_f\).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	\begin{proof}
189	
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
191	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
192	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
193	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
194	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
195	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
196	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
197	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
198	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
199	\end{proof}
200	
201	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
202	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
203	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
204	  \mathlibok
205	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
206	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
207	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
208	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
209	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
210	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
211	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
212	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
213	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
214	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
215	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
216	\end{lemma}
217	
218	\begin{lemma}[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
219	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
221	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
222	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
223	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
224	  exact sequence
225	  \[
226	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
227	  \]
228	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular \(i_f\) is injective and \(q_f\) is surjective.
229	\end{lemma}
230	
231	\begin{proof}
232	
233	  Localisation at a multiplicative set is an exact functor, preserving injections,
234	  surjections, and exactness. Applying it to the scalar-restricted \(A\)-linear maps
235	  \(i\) and \(q\) (via \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence. Proved directly in Lean.
236	\end{proof}
237	
238	\begin{lemma}[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
239	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
241	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
242	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
243	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
244	\end{lemma}
245	
246	\begin{proof}
247	
248	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
249	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
250	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
251	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
252	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
253	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
254	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
255	\end{proof}
256	
257	\begin{lemma}[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
258	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
259	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
260	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
261	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
262	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	
267	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
268	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
269	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
270	\end{proof}
271	
272	\begin{lemma}[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence (assembly)]
273	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
275	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
276	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
277	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark
278	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1737-L1745).
279	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
280	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
281	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
282	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
283	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
284	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form
285	  %  $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. This reduces us to the case where $B$
286	  %  is a domain and $M=B$."
287	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (splicing fact).}
288	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
289	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
290	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation
291	  \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
292	\end{lemma}
293	
294	\begin{proof}
295	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
296	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
297	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
298	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
299	  Set \(f := f' f''\); since \(A\) is a domain and \(f', f'' \ne 0\), the product is
300	  non-zero, so \(f \ne 0\). Localising the given short exact sequence at the powers
301	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
302	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
303	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
304	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the
305	  factorisation \(f = f' f''\) (using that \(M'_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\)) shows
306	  \(M'_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and applying the same lemma to \(M''\) with the
307	  factorisation \(f = f'' f'\) (using that \(M''_{f''}\) is free over \(A_{f''}\))
308	  shows \(M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Finally
309	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split} concludes that the middle term \(M_f\) is
310	  free over \(A_f\).
311	\end{proof}

313	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
314	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
315	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
316	  \mathlibok
317	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
318	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
319	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
320	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
321	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
322	\end{lemma}

324	\begin{lemma}
325	
326	[L4a — clearing one equation's denominators]
327	  \label{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_clear_one_denominator}
329	  % NOTE (shared engine): the denominator-clearing performed here -- pushing finitely
330	  % many coefficients of one polynomial from K = Frac(A) down into a single
331	  % Localization.Away g -- is the same primitive the torsion reindex (L5b,
332	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex) uses when it inverts the leading coefficient of the
333	  % Nagata-normalised annihilator. A prover who writes one general "clear finitely many
334	  % denominators into one Localization.Away g" helper can have both lemmas consume it.
335	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
336	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
338	  % "If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for
339	  %  coefficients of equations of integral dependence
340	  %  satisfied by a finite set of
341	  %  algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,
342	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
343	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
344	  %   Let K := FractionRing A and A_g := Localization.Away g; the canonical map
345	  %   A_g -> K (induced because powers of g are non-zero-divisors of A) extends along
346	  %   MvPolynomial.map to MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g -> MvPolynomial (Fin n) K.
347	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
348	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (FractionRing A)) :
349	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0),
350	  %         p ∈ Set.range
351	  %           (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)))
352	  % NOTE (iter-008 encoding): there is NO canonical `Algebra (Localization.Away g)
353	  %   (FractionRing A)` instance, so the landed Lean does not use the literal
354	  %   `algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)` above. It encodes the same map
355	  %   as `IsLocalization.map (FractionRing A) (RingHom.id A) hle` with
356	  %   `hle : powers g ≤ comap id (nonZeroDivisors A)` — mathematically identical, just the
357	  %   well-typed form. The proof is axiom-clean.
358	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
359	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
360	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
361	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
362	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
363	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
364	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
365	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
366	\end{lemma}
367	
368	\begin{proof}
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
370	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
371	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
372	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
373	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
374	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
375	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
376	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
377	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
378	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
379	\end{proof}

381	\begin{lemma}[L4 — Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators]
382	  \label{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}
383	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial}
384	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
385	  % NOTE (resolved iter-006): the LEAN SIGNATURE block below now matches the landed
386	  % Lean decl `exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial`, which RETAINS the binder
387	  % `(_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))` as
388	  % the 3rd existential (after `(_ : g ≠ 0)`, before `φ`) — required to type the
389	  % AlgHom target as an A_g-algebra. The MATH statement is unaffected.
390	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (landed target, AlgHom encoding). Write
391	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g,   B_g := Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g).
392	  % The conclusion is:
393	  %   ∃ (n : ℕ) (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
394	  %     (_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))
395	  %     (φ : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g)
396	  %           →ₐ[Localization.Away g] Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)),
397	  %     Function.Injective φ ∧
398	  %     (letI := φ.toAlgebra;
399	  %      Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g))
400	  %                    (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))
401	  % The polynomial subalgebra A_g[b_1,…,b_n] is realised as the DOMAIN
402	  % `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`, embedded into B_g by the algebra map φ; the
403	  % generators b_j are φ(X_j). Compared with an earlier draft using three anonymous
404	  % instance existentials (∃ (_ : Algebra A_g B_g) (_ : Algebra (MvPoly … A_g) B_g)
405	  % (_ : IsScalarTower …)), this form keeps only the single `Algebra A_g B_g` binder
406	  % needed to type B_g as an A_g-algebra: the single φ then carries the algebra map,
407	  % `Function.Injective φ` encodes algebraic independence of the b_j, and
408	  % `Module.Finite … over φ.toAlgebra` encodes module-finiteness of B_g over the
409	  % image polynomial subalgebra. Downstream, L5 (\cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core})
410	  % consumes a finite module over `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`; the AlgHom hands it
411	  % exactly that algebra structure via `φ.toAlgebra`, with no instance revival.
412	  % Why B_g and not B: generic flatness only needs the property after one
413	  % inversion g; the integral-dependence equations only clear denominators after
414	  % inverting g (see the SOURCE QUOTE PROOF below).
415	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
416	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
417	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
418	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
419	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
420	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
421	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
422	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
423	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
424	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
425	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
426	  normalisation with denominators).}
427	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
428	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
429	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
430	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
431	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
432	  \[
433	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
434	  \]
435	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
436	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
437	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
438	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
439	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
440	\end{lemma}
441	
442	\begin{proof}
443	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
444	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
445	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
446	  % NOTE (iter-007 decomposition): the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a
447	  % Finset-fold of lem:gf_clear_one_denominator over the finite generating set. Each
448	  % generator satisfies one equation of integral dependence whose coefficient
449	  % polynomials lie in K[b_1,...,b_n]; gf_clear_one_denominator clears the denominators
450	  % of one such coefficient polynomial into a single Localization.Away g_i. Folding over
451	  % the finite set of (generator, coefficient) pairs and multiplying the finitely many
452	  % g_i yields one common g != 0 over which all integrality relations hold; the AlgHom
453	  % assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
454	
455	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
456	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
457	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
458	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
459	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
460	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
461	
462	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
463	  finite set of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); each
464	  generator satisfies an equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are
465	  polynomials in \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\), and there are finitely many such
466	  coefficient polynomials in total. Applying \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} to each
467	  coefficient polynomial yields a non-zero element of \(A\) over which that polynomial's
468	  coefficients become integral; folding over the finite collection (multiplying the
469	  finitely many elements) produces a single \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that
470	  \emph{every} coefficient of \emph{every} integral-dependence equation lies in
471	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). After inverting \(g\) all these integrality relations hold
472	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the chosen generators generate \(B_g\) as a module
473	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
474	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
475	
476	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
477	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending
478	  \(X_j \mapsto b_j\); the algebraic independence of the \(b_j\) over \(A_g\) (inherited
479	  from their independence over \(K\)) makes \(\varphi\) injective, and the
480	  module-finiteness just established is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over the
481	  image of \(\varphi\).
482	\end{proof}

484	\begin{lemma}
485	
486	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
487	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
488	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
489	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
490	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
491	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
492	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
493	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
494	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
495	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
496	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
497	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
498	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
499	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
500	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
501	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
502	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
503	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
504	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
505	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
506	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
507	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
508	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
509	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
510	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
511	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
512	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
513	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
514	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
515	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
516	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
517	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
518	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
519	  \[
520	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
521	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
522	  \]
523	
524	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
525	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
526	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
527	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
528	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
529	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
530	\end{lemma}
531	
532	\begin{proof}
533	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
534	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
535	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
536	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
537	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
538	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
539	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
540	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
541	
542	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
543	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
544	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
545	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
546	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
547	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
548	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
549	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
550	
551	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
552	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
553	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
554	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
555	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
556	\end{proof}

586	\begin{lemma}[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
587	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
588	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
589	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
590	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
591	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
592	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
593	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
594	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
595	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
596	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
597	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A. The conclusion is the existence of a
598	  %   non-zero annihilator; the downstream consequence "T is finite over P_d/(F)" is
599	  %   left to the assembly (a surjection P_d ↠ P_d/(F) pushes a finite generating set).
600	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
601	  %       (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
602	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
603	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
604	  %       ∃ F : MvPolynomial (Fin d) A, F ≠ 0 ∧
605	  %         F ∈ Module.annihilator (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T
606	  %   (F ∈ annihilator unfolds to ∀ x : T, F • x = 0.)
607	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
608	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
609	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
610	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
611	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
612	\end{lemma}
613	
614	\begin{proof}
615	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
616	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
617	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
618	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
619	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
620	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
621	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
622	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
623	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
624	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
625	\end{proof}

627	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
628	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
629	
630	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
631	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
632	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
633	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
634	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
635	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
636	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
637	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
638	
639	\begin{definition}[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
640	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
641	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
642	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
643	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
644	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
645	  \[
646	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
647	  \]
648	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
649	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
650	\end{definition}
651	
652	\begin{lemma}[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
653	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
654	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
655	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
656	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
657	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
658	\end{lemma}
659	\begin{proof}
660	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
661	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
662	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\). Proved directly
663	  in Lean.
664	\end{proof}
665	
666	\begin{definition}[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
667	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
668	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
669	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
670	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
671	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
672	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
673	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
674	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
675	\end{definition}
676	
677	\begin{lemma}[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
678	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
679	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
680	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
681	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
682	\end{lemma}
683	\begin{proof}
684	  Proved directly in Lean.
685	\end{proof}
686	
687	\begin{lemma}[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
688	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
689	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
690	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
691	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
692	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
693	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
694	\end{lemma}
695	\begin{proof}
696	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
697	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
698	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
699	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
700	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
701	\end{proof}
702	
703	\begin{lemma}[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
704	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
705	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
706	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
707	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
708	  transformed monomial is the radix value
709	  \[
710	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
711	  \]
712	\end{lemma}
713	\begin{proof}
714	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
715	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
716	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
717	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
718	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
719	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
720	\end{proof}
721	
722	\begin{lemma}[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
723	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
724	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
725	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
726	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
727	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
728	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
729	\end{lemma}
730	\begin{proof}
731	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
732	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
733	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
734	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
735	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
736	\end{proof}
737	
738	\begin{lemma}[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
739	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
740	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
741	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
742	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
743	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
744	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
745	\end{lemma}
746	\begin{proof}
747	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
748	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
749	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
750	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
751	\end{proof}
752	
753	\begin{lemma}[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
754	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
755	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
756	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
757	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
758	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
759	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
760	\end{lemma}
761	\begin{proof}
762	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
763	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
764	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
765	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
766	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
767	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
768	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
769	\end{proof}
770	
771	\begin{lemma}[Naturality of the variable-splitting isomorphism under base change]
772	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
773	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
774	  For any ring homomorphism \(\varphi : A \to B\) and polynomial \(q \in A[X_0, \dots, X_m]\),
775	  the isomorphism \(A[X_0, \dots, X_m] \cong (A[X_1, \dots, X_m])[X_0]\) is natural with
776	  respect to applying \(\varphi\) coefficientwise.
777	\end{lemma}
778	\begin{proof}
779	  Proved directly in Lean by induction on \(q\).
780	\end{proof}
781	
782	\begin{lemma}[Constant inclusion under the variable-splitting isomorphism]
783	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
784	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
785	  For \(s \in R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), the composite of the inclusion
786	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) (sending \(X_j \mapsto X_j\))
787	  with the isomorphism \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) sends \(s\)
788	  to the constant polynomial \(s \in (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\).
789	\end{lemma}
790	\begin{proof}
791	  Proved directly in Lean by induction on \(s\).
792	\end{proof}

794	\begin{lemma}[L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables: monic in the distinguished variable]
795	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
796	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
797	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
798	  % NOTE (variable convention): the project's univariate elimination uses
799	  %   MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv, which singles out variable X_0 as the polynomial
800	  %   variable over the coefficient ring MvPolynomial (Fin n) A. The choice of which of
801	  %   the d = n+1 variables is "distinguished/eliminated" is immaterial (a renaming);
802	  %   the informal prose elsewhere says "last variable X_d" but the Lean uses X_0.
803	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
804	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
805	  % the Nagata substitution is the standard device realising the support-dimension drop.
806	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
807	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
808	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
809	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
810	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
811	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g; map := MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A A_g).
812	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
813	  %       (F : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A) (hF : F ≠ 0) :
814	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
815	  %         (e : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A ≃ₐ[A] MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A),
816	  %         IsUnit
817	  %           (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv (Localization.Away g) n
818	  %             (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A (Localization.Away g)) (e F))).leadingCoeff
819	  %   The automorphism e is the triangular Nagata substitution; g is the (non-zero)
820	  %   leading coefficient that becomes a unit after inversion.
821	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
822	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
823	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
824	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
825	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
826	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
827	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).
828	\end{lemma}
829	
830	\begin{proof}
831	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
832	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
833	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
834	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
835	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
836	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
837	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
838	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
839	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
840	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
841	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
842	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
843	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}

6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.

14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
