201	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
202	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
203	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
204	  \mathlibok
205	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
206	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
207	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
208	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
209	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
210	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
211	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
212	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
213	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
214	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
215	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
216	\end{lemma}

231	\begin{proof}
232	
233	  Localisation at a multiplicative set is an exact functor, preserving injections,
234	  surjections, and exactness. Applying it to the scalar-restricted \(A\)-linear maps
235	  \(i\) and \(q\) (via \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence. Proved directly in Lean.
236	\end{proof}

238	\begin{lemma}[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
239	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
241	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
242	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
243	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
244	\end{lemma}
245	
246	\begin{proof}
247	
248	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
249	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
250	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
251	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
252	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
253	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
254	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
255	\end{proof}

257	\begin{lemma}[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
258	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
259	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
260	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
261	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
262	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	
267	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
268	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
269	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
270	\end{proof}

360	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
361	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
362	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
363	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
364	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
365	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
366	\end{lemma}
367	
368	\begin{proof}
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
370	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
371	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
372	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
373	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
374	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
375	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
376	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
377	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
378	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
379	\end{proof}
380	
381	\begin{lemma}[L4 — Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators]
382	  \label{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}
383	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial}
384	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
385	  % NOTE (resolved iter-006): the LEAN SIGNATURE block below now matches the landed
386	  % Lean decl `exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial`, which RETAINS the binder
387	  % `(_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))` as
388	  % the 3rd existential (after `(_ : g ≠ 0)`, before `φ`) — required to type the
389	  % AlgHom target as an A_g-algebra. The MATH statement is unaffected.
390	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (landed target, AlgHom encoding). Write
391	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g,   B_g := Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g).
392	  % The conclusion is:
393	  %   ∃ (n : ℕ) (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
394	  %     (_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))
395	  %     (φ : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g)
396	  %           →ₐ[Localization.Away g] Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)),
397	  %     Function.Injective φ ∧
398	  %     (letI := φ.toAlgebra;
399	  %      Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g))
400	  %                    (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))
401	  % The polynomial subalgebra A_g[b_1,…,b_n] is realised as the DOMAIN
402	  % `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`, embedded into B_g by the algebra map φ; the
403	  % generators b_j are φ(X_j). Compared with an earlier draft using three anonymous
404	  % instance existentials (∃ (_ : Algebra A_g B_g) (_ : Algebra (MvPoly … A_g) B_g)
405	  % (_ : IsScalarTower …)), this form keeps only the single `Algebra A_g B_g` binder
406	  % needed to type B_g as an A_g-algebra: the single φ then carries the algebra map,
407	  % `Function.Injective φ` encodes algebraic independence of the b_j, and
408	  % `Module.Finite … over φ.toAlgebra` encodes module-finiteness of B_g over the
409	  % image polynomial subalgebra. Downstream, L5 (\cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core})
410	  % consumes a finite module over `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`; the AlgHom hands it
411	  % exactly that algebra structure via `φ.toAlgebra`, with no instance revival.
412	  % Why B_g and not B: generic flatness only needs the property after one
413	  % inversion g; the integral-dependence equations only clear denominators after
414	  % inverting g (see the SOURCE QUOTE PROOF below).
415	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
416	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
417	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
418	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
419	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
420	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
421	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
422	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
423	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
424	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
425	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
426	  normalisation with denominators).}
427	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
428	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
429	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
430	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
431	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
432	  \[
433	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
434	  \]
435	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
436	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
437	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
438	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
439	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
440	\end{lemma}
441	
442	\begin{proof}
443	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
444	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
445	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
446	  % NOTE (iter-007 decomposition): the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a
447	  % Finset-fold of lem:gf_clear_one_denominator over the finite generating set. Each
448	  % generator satisfies one equation of integral dependence whose coefficient
449	  % polynomials lie in K[b_1,...,b_n]; gf_clear_one_denominator clears the denominators
450	  % of one such coefficient polynomial into a single Localization.Away g_i. Folding over
451	  % the finite set of (generator, coefficient) pairs and multiplying the finitely many
452	  % g_i yields one common g != 0 over which all integrality relations hold; the AlgHom
453	  % assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
454	
455	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
456	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
457	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
458	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
459	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
460	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
461	
462	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
463	  finite set of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); each
464	  generator satisfies an equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are
465	  polynomials in \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\), and there are finitely many such
466	  coefficient polynomials in total. Applying \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} to each
467	  coefficient polynomial yields a non-zero element of \(A\) over which that polynomial's
468	  coefficients become integral; folding over the finite collection (multiplying the
469	  finitely many elements) produces a single \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that
470	  \emph{every} coefficient of \emph{every} integral-dependence equation lies in
471	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). After inverting \(g\) all these integrality relations hold
472	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the chosen generators generate \(B_g\) as a module
473	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
474	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
475	
476	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
477	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending
478	  \(X_j \mapsto b_j\); the algebraic independence of the \(b_j\) over \(A_g\) (inherited
479	  from their independence over \(K\)) makes \(\varphi\) injective, and the
480	  module-finiteness just established is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over the
481	  image of \(\varphi\).
482	\end{proof}
483	
484	\begin{lemma}
485	
486	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
487	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
488	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
489	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
490	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
491	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
492	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
493	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
494	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
495	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
496	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
497	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
498	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
499	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
500	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
501	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
502	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
503	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
504	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
505	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
506	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
507	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
508	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
509	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
510	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
511	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
512	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
513	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
514	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
515	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
516	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
517	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
518	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
519	  \[
520	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
521	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
522	  \]
523	
524	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
525	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
526	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
527	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
528	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
529	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
530	\end{lemma}
531	
532	\begin{proof}
533	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
534	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
535	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
536	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
537	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
538	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
539	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
540	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
541	
542	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
543	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
544	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
545	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
546	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
547	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
548	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
549	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
550	
551	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
552	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
553	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
554	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
555	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
556	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)

import re, sys

for path in [
    "blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex",

]:
    text = open(path).read()
    # Strip comments for env-balance check
    stripped_lines = []
    for line in text.splitlines():

        idx = line.find('%')
        if idx >= 0:
            stripped_lines.append(line[:idx])
        else:
            stripped_lines.append(line)
    stripped = '\n'.join(stripped_lines)
    
    begins = re.findall(r'\\begin\{([^}]+)\}', stripped)
    ends   = re.findall(r'\\end\{([^}]+)\}', stripped)
    
    from collections import Counter
    bc = Counter(begins)
    ec = Counter(ends)
    
    issues = []
    for env in set(list(bc.keys()) + list(ec.keys())):
        if bc.get(env, 0) != ec.get(env, 0):
            issues.append(f"  {env}: begin={bc.get(env,0)}, end={ec.get(env,0)}")
    
    name = path.split('/')[-1]
    if issues:
        print(f"IMBALANCED in {name}:")
        for i in issues: print(i)
    else:

EOF

2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and

7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).

499	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
500	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
501	
502	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
503	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
504	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
505	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
506	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
507	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
508	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
509	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
510	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
511	
512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
513	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
514	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
516	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
517	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
518	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
519	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
520	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
521	  \(F\):
522	  \[
523	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
524	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
525	  \]
526	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
527	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
528	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
529	  the Cauchy product.
530	\end{lemma}
531	
532	\begin{lemma}\leanok
533	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
534	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
535	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
536	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
537	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
538	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
539	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
540	  \[
541	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
542	  \]
543	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
544	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
545	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
546	  \[
547	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
548	  \]
549	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
550	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
551	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
552	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
553	\end{lemma}
554	
555	\begin{proof}
556	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
557	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
558	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
559	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
560	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
561	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
562	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
563	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
564	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
565	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
566	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
567	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
568	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
569	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
570	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
571	\end{proof}
572	
573	\begin{definition}\leanok
574	[Rational Hilbert function]
575	  \label{def:ratHilb}
576	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
577	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
578	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
579	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
580	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
581	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
582	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
583	  \[
584	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
585	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
586	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
587	  \]

589	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that

592	\end{definition}

594	\begin{lemma}\leanok
595	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
596	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
597	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
598	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
599	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
600	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
601	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
602	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
603	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
604	\end{lemma}
605	
606	\begin{lemma}\leanok
607	[Raising the pole order]
608	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
609	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
610	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
611	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
612	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
613	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
614	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
615	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
616	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
617	\end{lemma}
618	
619	\begin{lemma}\leanok
620	[Closure under difference]
621	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
622	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
623	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
624	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
625	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
626	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
627	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
628	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
629	\end{lemma}
630	
631	\begin{lemma}\leanok
632	[Closure under right shift]
633	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
634	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
635	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
636	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
637	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
638	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
639	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
640	  Lean.
641	\end{lemma}
642	
643	\begin{lemma}\leanok
644	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
645	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
646	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
647	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
648	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
649	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
The prose looks clean. Let me now read a bit more of QuotScheme to check the subquotient machinery blocks and any potential issues there.

650	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
651	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
652	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
653	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
654	\end{lemma}
655	
656	\begin{proof}
657	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
658	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
659	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
660	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
661	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
662	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
663	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
664	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
665	\end{proof}

667	\begin{lemma}\leanok
668	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
669	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
670	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
671	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
672	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
673	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
674	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
675	  \[
676	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
677	  \]
678	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
679	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
680	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
681	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
682	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
683	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
684	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
685	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
686	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
687	\end{lemma}
688	
689	\begin{proof}
690	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
691	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
692	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
693	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
694	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
695	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
696	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
697	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
698	\end{proof}

700	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
701	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
702	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
703	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
704	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
705	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
706	  % Polynomials" (read from
707	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
708	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
709	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
710	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
711	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
712	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
713	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
714	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
715	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
716	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
717	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
718	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
719	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
720	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
721	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
722	  \[
723	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
724	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
725	  \]
726	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
727	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
728	\end{theorem}
729	
730	\begin{proof}
731	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
732	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
733	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
734	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
735	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
736	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
737	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
738	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
739	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
740	  function
741	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
742	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
743	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
744	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
745	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
746	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
747	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
748	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
749	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
750	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
751	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
752	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
753	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
754	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
755	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
756	\end{proof}

758	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
759	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
760	
761	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
762	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
763	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
764	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
765	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
766	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
767	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
768	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
769	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
770	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
771	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
772	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
773	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
774	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
775	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
776	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
777	
778	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
779	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
780	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
781	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
782	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
783	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
784	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
785	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
786	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
787	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
788	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
789	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
790	
791	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
792	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
793	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
794	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
795	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
796	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
797	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
798	\(x \in R_1\).
799	
800	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}
801	
802	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
803	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
804	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
805	  \mathlibok
806	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
807	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
808	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
809	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
810	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
811	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
812	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
813	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure; the ambient internal
814	  direct-sum decomposition \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\) is supplied by the split
815	  \(G_1\) (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
816	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}).
817	\end{lemma}
818	
819	\begin{lemma}[A span of homogeneous elements is homogeneous]
820	  \label{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}
821	  \lean{Ideal.homogeneous_span}
822	  \mathlibok
823	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
824	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Ideal}).}
825	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(s \subseteq R\) a set each of
826	  whose elements is homogeneous. Then the ideal \(\mathrm{span}\,s\) is a
827	  homogeneous ideal. In particular, for \(x \in R_1\) the principal ideal \((x)\)
828	  is homogeneous, hence \(xM\) and the auxiliary submodule \(N' + x \cdot N\) are
829	  homogeneous; this homogeneity feeds the kernel/cokernel closure
830	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
831	\end{lemma}
832	
833	\begin{lemma}[Rank--nullity]
834	  \label{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}
835	  \lean{LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker}
836	  \mathlibok
837	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
838	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.FiniteDimensional.Lemmas}).}
839	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
840	  \(\varphi : V \to W\) a \(\kappa\)-linear map. Then
841	  \[
842	    \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
843	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
844	  \]
845	  This is the rank--nullity identity underlying the degreewise difference identity
846	  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}).
847	\end{lemma}

849	\begin{lemma}[Finite generation transfers along a surjection]

863	\begin{lemma}[Multiplication by a homogeneous element shifts degree]
864	  \label{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
865	  \lean{SetLike.IsHomogeneousElem.graded_smul}
866	  \mathlibok

868	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a graded family acting on a graded family \(\mathcal{M}\)

878	\subsubsection*{The internal grading of a homogeneous submodule (split $G_1$)}
879	
880	\begin{lemma}[$G_1$ (independence) -- the family $n \mapsto M_n \cap p$ is independent]
881	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}
882	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep}
883	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
884	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and let
885	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
886	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
887	  \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces of \(M\) is independent: for each
888	  \(n\),
889	  \[
890	    (M_n \cap p) \;\cap\; \Bigl(\bigsqcup_{m \ne n} (M_m \cap p)\Bigr) \;=\; 0 .
891	  \]
892	  The whole statement lives in the ambient module \(M\): the subspaces
893	  \(M_n \cap p\) are submodules of \(M\), never of a derived carrier \(\smash{\hat p}\).
894	\end{lemma}
895	
896	\begin{proof}
897	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
898	  The components \(M_n\) of the internal decomposition of \(M\) are independent. The
899	  subfamily \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) consists of submodules with \(M_n \cap p \le M_n\),
900	  so independence is inherited: a relation among the \(M_n \cap p\) is in particular a
901	  relation among the \(M_n\), forcing each term to vanish.
902	\end{proof}
903	
904	\begin{lemma}[$G_1$ (supremum) -- $\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) = p$ for $p$ homogeneous]
905	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
906	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_inf_eq}
907	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
908	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
909	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then
910	  \[
911	    \bigsqcup_{n} (M_n \cap p) \;=\; p .
912	  \]
913	  Together with the independence of
914	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}, this is the ambient internal
915	  direct sum \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\), stated entirely inside \(M\) (no
916	  \(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\) on the subtype \(\smash{\hat p}\)).
917	\end{lemma}
918	
919	\begin{proof}
920	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
921	  The inclusion \(\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) \le p\) is clear, since each
922	  \(M_n \cap p \le p\). For the reverse inclusion, take \(m \in p\) and write
923	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) using the
924	  internal decomposition of \(M\). Homogeneity of \(p\) gives
925	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\), hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n \cap p\); thus
926	  \(m\) is a finite sum of elements of the \(M_n \cap p\), i.e.
927	  \(m \in \bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p)\).
928	\end{proof}

930	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient class and its Hilbert function}
931	
932	\begin{definition}[Subquotient Hilbert data]
933	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
934	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
935	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
936	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
937	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e. \(M\) together with an
938	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
939	  (\(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\ \mathcal{M}\)) whose components
940	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
941	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
942	  \begin{itemize}
943	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
944	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
945	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
946	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
947	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
948	    \item a finiteness witness that \(M\) is a finite module over the polynomial
949	      ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\)
950	      (\(\mathrm{Module.Finite}\)).
951	  \end{itemize}
952	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
953	  Hilbert function} is
954	  \[
955	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
956	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
957	  \]
958	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
959	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
960	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
961	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
962	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
963	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
964	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
965	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
966	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
967	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
968	\end{definition}
969	
970	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
971	
972	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
973	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
974	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_ker_coker}
975	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
976	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
977	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
978	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
979	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
980	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
981	  \[
982	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
983	    \qquad
984	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
985	  \]
986	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
987	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
988	  \begin{enumerate}
989	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
990	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
991	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
992	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
993	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
994	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
995	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
996	  \end{enumerate}
997	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
998	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
999	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1000	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1001	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1002	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1003	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1004	\end{lemma}
1005	
1006	\begin{proof}
1007	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1008	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1009	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
1010	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1011	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1012	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1013	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1014	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1015	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1016	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1017	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1018	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1019	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1020	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1021	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1022	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1023	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1024	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1025	  immediate.
1026	
1027	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1028	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1029	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1030	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1031	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1032	
1033	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1034	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1035	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1036	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1037	
1038	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1039	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1040	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1041	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1042	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1043	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1044	\end{proof}
1045	
1046	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1047	
1048	\begin{lemma}[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1049	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1050	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1051	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1052	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1053	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1054	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}

1056	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1057	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1058	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1059	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1060	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1061	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1062	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1063	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1064	  \[
1065	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1066	  \]
1067	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1068	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1069	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1070	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1071	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1072	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1073	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1074	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1075	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1076	\end{lemma}
1077	
1078	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1079	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1080	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1081	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does

1089	\begin{proof}

1097	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)

1099	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
